title: Chapter 27: The Greenhouse Revisited
content: The door stuck. Of course it did — everything in this place had been surrendering to rot for years. I put my shoulder into it, and the rusted hinge screamed before giving way, the sound torn apart by wind before it could echo.

I stepped inside and the smell hit me first. Dead leaves and wet soil and something sweetness-rotten underneath, the ghost of tomatoes left to split on the vine. The greenhouse had always smelled alive — humid and green and thick with growth. Now it smelled like a funeral for something that never got a proper burial.

The glass was worse than I remembered. Most of the western wall had fractured into spiderwebs, and whole panes on the south face were missing, replaced by ragged holes that let the November air carve through in steady currents. The wind made a low, fluting sound as it moved through the cracks — not quite a whistle, more like breathing through broken teeth.

I walked the central aisle anyway. The planters were still there, their wooden frames gray and splintered, their soil now home to weeds that had gone feral and then died back with the frost. And there — the long planter near the back, the one where we'd pressed our fingers into the dirt and made parallel lines.
I could still see them.

Weathered, yes. Smudged by rain and time and neglect. But two lines running side by side, close enough to touch, never crossing. Her line and mine. I'd thought it was a joke at the time. She'd said it was a promise.
I sat on the edge of the planter. The wood was cold through my coat, a dull ache settling into my thighs. I let it come — the cold, the ache. Let the memories come too.
Not the bad ones. I'd earned that much, at least. The discipline to choose what I remembered.

I chose the way she taught me to read a room. Not the clinical version — watch the exits, clock the power players, find the leverage. She'd taught me the other kind. The one where you feel the temperature shift before someone raises their voice. The one where you know a smile is a weapon before it lands. *"You don't read rooms, Heath. You read the people who think they're hiding in them."* She'd said it like it was obvious. Like it was something I should have known about myself all along.

I chose the way she traced my scar. Not with pity — never with pity. She'd run her fingertip along the ridge of tissue above my eyebrow like she was reading braille, and when she was done, she'd said: *"That's not damage. That's a weapon. It means you've already survived something that tried to kill you."* And for the first time, I'd believed it. Not because the words were true but because she'd made them true by saying them.
I chose the way she made being broken feel like art.

That was the one that undid me. Not the pain — I knew pain, had a working relationship with it. But the way she looked at my jagged edges and didn't see something to fix. Saw something to display. Something with value precisely because it hadn't been smoothed down.
The wind pushed through a crack above my head and I heard it — a thin, high sound, almost a note. The greenhouse was still making music. Broken, but singing.
I started the inventory in my head. I'd been doing it a lot lately, a mental ledger that never quite balanced.

Lost: Three years with her. The simplicity of being her weapon. The clean, bright certainty of having exactly one purpose — stand between Cat and the world, absorb the damage, keep moving. When you're a weapon, you don't have to think about what you want. The wielder decides. It's almost peaceful, in a terrifying way.
Lost: The version of myself who could exist in her orbit without burning up. The Heath who walked into her gravity and stayed there because escaping seemed worse than circling forever.

Gained: Agency. The word felt awkward in my mouth even now, like a language I was still learning. I had it — real, functional, mine. I could make decisions that weren't about her. I could build things that weren't shields.

Gained: Power. Not the kind Robert had — hoarded, brittle, dependent on fear. The kind that comes from knowing you can walk away and choosing not to. The kind that comes from being hard to break and impossible to control.
Gained: The ability to meet her as an equal.

That one knocked the air out of me every time I thought it. Because it was true. I could stand across from Catriona Aldana now and not feel the ground tilt. I could hold her gaze without looking for permission in it. I could want her without needing her to want me back in order to survive.

I was harder in some ways — the blizzard had seen to that, and the months after. Harder in the places that used to bend for her. But softer in the ones that mattered. Softer in the place where I kept the knowledge that I could walk away and still be whole.
I no longer needed her to survive.
I chose her.
That was different. That was everything.

The notebook was in my coat pocket, where it always was. Leather cover, worn soft at the corners, pages dense with crossed-out entries and debt calculations and the kind of emotional accounting that would have made a therapist weep.

I pulled it out and flipped past the old entries — the ones where I'd tried to calculate what I owed her, what she owed me, where the balance sat. Past the pages where I'd spiraled into self-recrimination. Past the lists of people I'd have to destroy to keep her safe.
A blank page near the back. Almost the last one. I uncapped my pen and wrote:
*Cruz, Heathcliff. Status: Uncertain. Asset: Everything I've built. Liability: Her.*
My hand stopped. The pen hovered over the page, and I felt the truth of it rising in my throat like bile or confession — same thing, sometimes. I kept writing.
*Not because she'll hurt me — because I'll give it all up if she asks. And that's the most dangerous thing I've ever been.*

I stared at the entry. The words didn't look like much. Black ink on off-white paper, my handwriting cramped the way it always was when I was being honest — like even my fingers wanted to take it back.
But it was the most honest thing I'd ever written.

More honest than the debt ledger, because those numbers were just armor. More honest than the crossed-out entries, because those were attempts at lying to myself that even I couldn't sustain. This — this was the raw nerve, exposed and pulsing.

I was a liability to myself. Not because of what Cat might do to me but because of what I'd do for her. The distinction mattered. She'd never asked me to sacrifice anything. That was the problem. She didn't have to ask. I'd hand it over before the question left her mouth.
And the most dangerous thing wasn't that she might take everything. It was that I'd give it gladly, and then what would be left of me?
I turned the page. I didn't tear the entry out. That would have been another kind of lie.
I'd been circling it for weeks. The realization. It had been pacing the edges of my mind like something caged, waiting for me to stop running long enough to look at it directly.
The conspiracy wasn't enough.

Burning Blackwood to the ground — sure. Strategy. Necessary. Destroying Robert, stripping him of the power he'd used to turn people into props and leverage and collateral damage. Also necessary. I could plan that in my sleep, had been planning it in my sleep, dreaming in boardroom maneuvers and poison-pill clauses.
But after the fire — what was left?

If it was just ruins and revenge, if it was just two people standing in the ashes of the thing that had made them, then Robert won. Because his philosophy would have survived even if his empire hadn't. The idea that people are tools. That love is leverage. That the only relationship worth having is one where you hold the knife and someone else holds still.
I needed Cat to want more than destruction.

The thought was terrifying. Not because I didn't think she was capable of it — Catriona Aldana was capable of anything she decided to be. But because wanting more than destruction meant admitting you believed in something beyond the burn. It meant vulnerability. It meant standing in front of someone with nothing in your hands and saying: *This is me. No strategy. No angle. Just me.*
I needed her to want a future. Our future.

Not as weapon and wielder — we'd played that game and I knew every room in that house, every exit, every shadow. Not as co-conspirators — that was just the weapon-and-wielder dynamic wearing a nicer suit.
Two people who choose each other when the smoke clears.
That was what I wanted. That was what I was afraid to name, because naming it meant I couldn't pretend it was anything less.
The fear came next. It always did.

Not the blizzard — I'd walked into that with my eyes open and come out the other side with frostbite and clarity. Not the board — Robert's board was formidable, but boards could be dismantled, their members turned, their structures exposed. Not even Robert himself, though he'd earned his place in my nightmares.
Vulnerability.

Offering Cat something real meant I had to be real too. Not the version of real I'd been performing for years — the scarred soldier, the loyal blade, the man who existed in relation to her and nothing else. Actually real. The Heath who wanted things he couldn't calculate the cost of. The Heath who might not get what he was asking for.
And being real meant she could hurt me.

Not the way a fist hurts. Not the way a financial ruin hurts. Those I knew how to carry — I'd been carrying them my whole life, the weight familiar enough to be almost comfortable. No. She could hurt me the way only someone who's seen you naked in every sense can hurt you. The way that leaves a mark no one else can see but you feel every time you breathe.
I thought about the Mexican Prop.

That night. Robert's voice through the door, smooth and certain, explaining to someone that people were just positions to be filled and Heath was performing adequately in his. The word *prop* — the way it had settled into my chest like a splinter I couldn't extract.
I'd thought, after that night: never again. Never again will I be someone's prop. Never again will I stand in someone else's scene and say the lines they've written for me.
But love makes you a prop.

The thought arrived unwelcome and refused to leave. Love makes you willing to stand in someone else's scene. To be whatever they need — shield, weapon, witness. Not because you've been cast but because you've chosen the role. Because their scene is where you want to be, even if the script is still being written.
The question wasn't whether I was willing. I'd already answered that a hundred times over, with my body, with my choices, with that entry in the notebook.
The question was: was Cat willing to do the same?
Was she willing to step into my scene? To be not the director, not the architect, but just a person standing next to me with nothing in her hands and no plan in her eyes?
I didn't know. And not knowing was the worst part — worse than any certainty, even bad certainty. Because it meant the answer could go either way, and I'd have to live with whichever one she chose.

I stood. The movement sent dead leaves scattering from my coat — brittle, brown things that crumbled against the stone floor and released another wave of that sweet rot, the greenhouse giving up its ghosts one breath at a time. I brushed the rest from my sleeves, from my shoulders, felt them crackle under my palms.

I walked toward the door, and that's when I saw it — the light. The late afternoon sun hitting the cracked glass at just the right angle, throwing fractured patterns across the floor. Not whole light. Broken into a dozen jagged pieces, each one shifted slightly from the others. But still light. Still finding its way through.
Broken, but still letting it in.
I stopped in the doorway and took out my phone. My fingers were cold and the screen glowed too bright against the dim interior. I opened a new message to Cat.
*After the gala. You and me. No strategy. No conspiracy. Just us. Can you do that?*
I read it three times. Then I read it again. Each word felt like a step out over open air — no net, no rope, just the conviction that the other side existed and I could reach it.
I stared at the message for a full minute. The cursor blinked. The wind moved through the broken glass behind me, and somewhere in the greenhouse, a loose pane rattled in its frame.
I pressed send.
The message went. A single gray check mark in a white bubble, floating between us like something fragile and untested.
I put the phone back in my pocket and stepped out into the cold. The greenhouse door groaned shut behind me, and I didn't look back.
The question wasn't whether Cat would say yes.
The question was whether either of us knew how to exist without the game. And whether we'd be willing to learn.
